title: Life Insurance for Homeowners in Canada
seo_title: Life Insurance for Homeowners Canada | Protect Your Mortgage | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Life insurance for homeowners in Canada. Protect your mortgage and family's home. Why personal term life beats bank mortgage insurance. Ontario advisors.

secondary_keywords: protecting your home with life insurance
_meta.conversion_goal: Lead capture — homeowners comparing bank mortgage insurance vs. personal coverage. Get them to compare quotes.
_meta.page_role: Demographic page targeting homeowners. Complements mortgage-protection.json. More empathetic framing around protecting the family home.
_meta.writer_focus: Homeowners want to protect their home for their family. Frame this around the asset they've worked for — not death. The message: 'You've worked hard for your home. Make sure your family keeps it.' Tie coverage amount directly to mortgage balance.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'life insurance for homeowners' 2–3 times per 500 words. Use 'mortgage protection' naturally throughout.
action_keywords: insure your home for your family
natural_variations: keeping your family in their home
what covers your mortgage if you die
protecting your home purchase with life insurance
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Sarah and Tom, Mississauga, $720K mortgage, 2 children. Both got 20-year term: Tom $750K for $68/month, Sarah $750K for $54/month. Either death: mortgage paid, family stays in home.
seo_keywords.avoid: Don't repeat the full bank vs. term insurance argument from mortgage-protection.json — keep it brief here and link to that page for detail.

page_data.heading: Life Insurance for Homeowners: Keep Your Family in Their Home
page_data.subheading: You've worked hard to own your home. A 20-year term life policy ensures your family never has to sell it because they can't afford the mortgage.

_writer_notes.focus: Emotional connection to the home they've worked for. Protection framing — not fear.
key_points: Coverage tied directly to mortgage balance
Personal term life beats bank mortgage insurance in almost every measure

page_data.heading: What Happens to Your Mortgage If You Die?

_writer_notes.focus: Explain what happens without coverage — and with coverage. No jargon.
key_points: Without life insurance: your estate must settle debts. Surviving spouse may have to sell the home if they can't carry the mortgage alone.
With life insurance: death benefit goes to your beneficiary (spouse/family). They can pay off mortgage, stay in home, or choose how to use funds.
With bank mortgage insurance: payout goes directly to the bank, not your family. Benefit decreases as you pay down mortgage. Premiums don't decrease.
With personal term life: your family decides what to do with the money. Full, level benefit throughout the term.

page_data.heading: How Much Life Insurance Does a Homeowner Need?

_writer_notes.focus: Coverage calculation for homeowners. Start with mortgage balance, then add income.
key_points: Minimum: your outstanding mortgage balance (so family can pay it off)
Better: mortgage + 5–10 years of income replacement (so surviving spouse can maintain lifestyle)
Best: DIME method — full needs assessment (see calculator page)
On a $700K mortgage with a $90K income and 2 kids: recommended coverage = $1.2–1.5M for primary earner
On a $400K mortgage, both spouses working, kids grown: $500–$700K each may be sufficient

page_data.heading: Personal Term Life vs. Bank Mortgage Insurance: The Short Version

_writer_notes.focus: Brief comparison — link to full mortgage-protection page for detail.
key_points: Bank mortgage insurance: benefit decreases as you pay off mortgage; premiums stay the same; bank is beneficiary
Personal term life: fixed benefit regardless of mortgage balance; your family is beneficiary; portable
Cost: bank mortgage insurance often costs more per dollar of coverage than personal term life
Winner: personal term life in almost all cases for healthy Canadians under 60
_writer_notes.callout_after: Link: 'Read the full comparison: Bank Mortgage Insurance vs. Personal Term Life Insurance'

page_data.heading: How Long Should Your Coverage Last?

_writer_notes.focus: Match term length to mortgage payoff timeline.
key_points: Match your term to your mortgage amortization: 25-year mortgage? Consider a 25 or 30-year term.
20-year term is the most popular choice — covers most of working life and mortgage paydown
Some homeowners overlap terms: 10-year + 20-year stacked to reduce coverage as mortgage decreases
At renewal: reassess — if mortgage is paid off and kids are grown, you may not need as much coverage

page_data.heading: Sample Rates: Life Insurance for Homeowners

_writer_notes.focus: Rates for common homeowner scenarios. $600K–$800K 20-year term.
key_points: Age 32, male, $700K 20-year term: ~$58–$78/month
Age 32, female, $700K 20-year term: ~$44–$60/month
Age 38, male, $600K 20-year term: ~$60–$80/month
Age 38, female, $600K 20-year term: ~$47–$65/month
Age 43, male, $500K 20-year term: ~$70–$90/month
Comparison: bank mortgage insurance on $700K mortgage often $140–$190/month with decreasing benefit

faqs.question: Should I get life insurance before or after buying a home?
faqs.answer: Ideally, you should have life insurance in place before or as soon as you take possession of your home. If you close on a purchase and don't yet have personal coverage, apply immediately. The mortgage creates an instant significant financial obligation.
faqs._guidance: Timing question — many readers are recent buyers.

faqs.question: Does life insurance pay off my mortgage when I die?
faqs.answer: With a personal term life policy, the death benefit is paid to your named beneficiary. They can use the funds to pay off the mortgage if they choose — but they're not required to. With bank mortgage insurance, the payout goes directly to the lender to retire the mortgage balance.

faqs.question: Can I get life insurance if my home has a second mortgage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Second mortgages and HELOCs are simply additional debt. Include the total outstanding balance in your coverage calculation. Some people hold separate policies or separate riders to cover each debt.
faqs._guidance: Practical question for homeowners with layered debt.

faqs.question: What happens to my life insurance if I sell my home?
faqs.answer: Nothing — your personal term life policy continues unchanged. It's not tied to the property. Bank mortgage insurance, however, is tied to the mortgage and ends when the mortgage is paid off or you move lenders. This is one of the key advantages of personal coverage.
faqs._guidance: Reinforce portability advantage of personal term.
